headline: DAO-governed with a dynamic Dual Governance timelock, but fast-path execution delay is under 7 days.

findings.text: Lido documentation states the use of 'OssifiableProxy' patterns for core contract upgradeability, though exact on-chain implementations and admins were not directly read from storage in this run.

Lido's Dual Governance architecture acts as a dynamic timelock between Aragon DAO votes and execution. Uncontested proposals face a minimum delay of 3 to 5 days before they can be scheduled and executed, varying by opposition levels.

Lido delegates specific powers to various multisigs, including a 3/6 GateSeal committee (0x8772E3a2D86B9347A2688f9bc1808A6d8917760C), a 4/6 CSM GateSeal committee (0xC52fC3081123073078698F1EAc2f1Dc7Bd71880f), and Emergency Activation (4/7) / Execution (5/7) committees for Dual Governance protection.
Core on-chain governance is managed via Aragon. Documented parameters stipulate a 5-day on-chain voting period requiring a >50% approval threshold and a >5% total token supply quorum.

Emergency pause mechanics are handled via 'GateSeals' — one-time panic button smart contracts that allow designated committees to pause specific protocol contracts (e.g., the Withdrawal Queue and Validator Exit Bus) for an 11-day period to react to vulnerabilities.

steelman.red: Emergency multisig committees retain the capability to pause critical withdrawal components, and without complete verifiable identities, these privileged roles pose a centralization risk.
steelman.orange: Despite its robust Dual Governance dynamic timelock and active DAO, the uncontested fast-path delay between a successful vote and execution is under 7 days, and multisig signers' insider statuses are not fully proven.
steelman.green: Lido has pioneered a highly decentralized Dual Governance model that empowers stETH holders to veto LDO tokenholder decisions, dynamically extending the timelock up to 45 days in case of malicious upgrades.

rationale.verdict: Choosing orange because while Lido implements sophisticated on-chain governance (Aragon) and user-protective dynamic timelocks (Dual Governance), the uncontested fast-path execution delay is less than the 7-day standard, and the insider/outsider composition of its various emergency multisigs cannot be definitively verified to meet the Security Council standard.

evidence.shows: Deployed Lido Dual Governance Mainnet contract block explorer page.
Documentation of Lido's core protocol deployed contracts, proxies, and Dual Governance committee addresses.
Details regarding the 5-day Aragon on-chain voting period, quorum requirements, and the minimum 3-day Dual Governance timelock for uncontested proposals.
LIP-28 specifications detailing the Dual Governance dynamic timelock mechanics, emergency activation, and execution committees.
Governance discussion and parameters outlining the GateSeal multisig addresses, signers, and the 11-day duration pause power over withdrawal components.

unknowns: C1: Exact admin/owner addresses for core proxy contracts verified directly via on-chain state reads.
C2: Explicit proxy upgrade pattern verified directly via on-chain storage reads.
C3: Exact timelock delay constants in seconds read directly from Dual Governance on-chain slots.
C4: Individual identities and insider/outsider affiliations of the signers for the GateSeal and Emergency committees to assess Security Council qualifications.
C5: Exact governance proposal threshold and quorum percentages read directly from Aragon on-chain slots.

protocol_metadata.about: Lido is a decentralized liquid staking protocol. It allows users to stake assets like ETH and receive liquid staking tokens (such as stETH) in return, which represent the staked principal and accrued rewards. By pooling and delegating these assets to professional node operators, Lido secures Proof-of-Stake networks while enabling users to remain liquid and participate in the broader DeFi ecosystem.
